content.text: Google SRE introduced the four golden signals as the minimum set of metrics that, if monitored, give you the earliest possible warning of a service degradation: latency, traffic, errors, and saturation. The framework is powerful because it covers failure modes from all directions — slow responses, high load, fault rates, and resource exhaustion — with a small number of metrics that are actionable rather than decorative.

GenAI services fit the framework but require significant adaptation. A traditional web service has one latency signal and one traffic signal. A GenAI service has several latency signals (time to first token versus end-to-end time), token volume as a distinct traffic signal independent of request count, a richer error taxonomy (provider errors, safety blocks, quality failures), and an unusual saturation resource: provider rate limits rather than CPU or memory.

If you can only instrument four things, instrument the four golden signals adapted for your service's specific failure modes. Everything else — cost metrics, quality metrics, trace-level detail — comes after these are solid.

attrs.alt: The four golden signals adapted for GenAI services

content.text: Latency for a GenAI service is not a single number. Streaming and non-streaming responses have different user experience models and require different SLOs. A streaming chat interface is acceptable at 30 seconds total if the first token arrives in 500 ms; the same 30-second response in a batch API endpoint would be a timeout.

TTFT is the interval from when the service sends the inference request to when the first response token is received. It is the primary latency SLI for any streaming interface because it determines when the user sees a response start. A fast TTFT makes a slow generation feel responsive; a slow TTFT makes a fast generation feel broken.

from opentelemetry import metrics, trace
from opentelemetry.trace import SpanKind
import time
import openai


meter = metrics.get_meter("genai-service")
tracer = trace.get_tracer("genai-service")

ttft_histogram = meter.create_histogram(
    "genai_chat_ttft_seconds",
    description="Time from request send to first token received (streaming only)",
    unit="s",
)

e2e_histogram = meter.create_histogram(
    "genai_chat_request_duration_seconds",
    description="End-to-end request duration including TTFT and generation",
    unit="s",
)


def chat_streaming(messages: list[dict], model: str, **base_tags) -> str:
    client = openai.OpenAI()
    start = time.monotonic()
    first_token_time: float | None = None
    chunks = []

    with tracer.start_as_current_span("gen_ai chat", kind=SpanKind.CLIENT) as span:
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.system", "openai")
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", model)

        stream = client.chat.completions.create(
            model=model, messages=messages, stream=True
        )

        for chunk in stream:
            delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content or ""
            if delta and first_token_time is None:
                first_token_time = time.monotonic()
                ttft_s = first_token_time - start
                ttft_histogram.record(ttft_s, {**base_tags, "model": model})
                span.add_event("gen_ai.first_token",
                               attributes={"ttft_seconds": ttft_s})
            chunks.append(delta)

        e2e_s = time.monotonic() - start
        e2e_histogram.record(e2e_s, {**base_tags, "model": model, "stream": "true"})

    return "".join(chunks)

Request-per-second is a useful traffic metric for traditional services where each request has similar computational cost. For GenAI services, a request with a 200-token prompt and a 50-token response consumes 1/50th the provider quota of a request with a 5,000-token prompt and a 3,000-token response. Token throughput is the true traffic signal.

from opentelemetry import metrics


meter = metrics.get_meter("genai-service")

# Request-level traffic
request_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "genai_chat_request_total",
    description="Total chat completion requests",
)

# Token-level traffic — this is the real load signal
token_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "genai_chat_completion_tokens_total",
    description="Cumulative tokens consumed (input + output separated by token_type tag)",
)

# Concurrent streaming connections — measures server-side load
active_streams_gauge = meter.create_up_down_counter(
    "genai_chat_active_streams",
    description="Currently open streaming response connections",
)

# Queue depth for async inference
queue_depth_gauge = meter.create_up_down_counter(
    "genai_inference_queue_depth",
    description="Pending inference requests waiting for a concurrency slot",
)


def record_traffic(
    input_tokens: int,
    output_tokens: int,
    model: str,
    provider: str,
    env: str,
) -> None:
    base = {"model": model, "provider": provider, "env": env}
    request_counter.add(1, base)
    token_counter.add(input_tokens, {**base, "token_type": "input"})
    token_counter.add(output_tokens, {**base, "token_type": "output"})

Token throughput drives two separate concerns: provider rate limit utilisation (a saturation signal) and cost accumulation (a business signal). Tracking both separately avoids conflating an operational problem with a financial one in incident triage.
The error taxonomy for GenAI services is richer than for typical APIs because some failures are invisible to HTTP status codes. A response with status 200 that has
is a functional failure from the user's perspective even though no exception was thrown.

from opentelemetry import metrics
from enum import Enum


class GenAIErrorType(str, Enum):
    RATE_LIMIT = "rate_limit"
    TIMEOUT = "timeout"
    MODEL_UNAVAILABLE = "model_unavailable"
    AUTH_ERROR = "auth_error"
    VALIDATION = "validation"
    CONTENT_FILTER = "content_filter"   # safety block
    LENGTH = "length"                    # token budget exhaustion
    EVAL_THRESHOLD = "eval_threshold"    # quality gate failure
    UNKNOWN = "unknown"


meter = metrics.get_meter("genai-service")

error_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "genai_chat_error_total",
    description="Total failed or functionally incomplete chat completions",
)


def record_error(
    error_type: GenAIErrorType,
    model: str,
    provider: str,
    env: str,
    http_status: int = 0,
) -> None:
    error_counter.add(1, {
        "error_type": error_type.value,
        "model": model,
        "provider": provider,
        "env": env,
        "http_status": str(http_status),
    })


def is_functional_error(finish_reason: str) -> bool:
    """
    True if a 200 OK response represents a functional failure.
    These count toward the error rate SLO.
    """
    return finish_reason in {"length", "content_filter"}

Saturation measures how close a service is to its capacity ceiling. For a traditional service, saturation is CPU utilisation or memory pressure. For a managed GenAI service, the primary saturation resource is the provider's rate limit — measured in tokens per minute (TPM) or requests per minute (RPM). When this ceiling is approached, latency degrades before hard errors appear, making it a leading indicator.
Alert Rules for All Four Signals

Each golden signal generates at least one alert rule. Alert rules should fire before the SLO is breached, not after. The burn rate alert model (alerting when error budget consumption rate is too high) is preferable to simple threshold alerts on individual metrics.

# Prometheus/Thanos alert rules for GenAI golden signals
groups:
  - name: genai-golden-signals
    rules:

      # --- LATENCY ---
      - alert: GenAIChatTTFTHigh
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.99,
            rate(genai_chat_ttft_seconds_bucket{env="production"}[5m])
          ) > 2.0
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "GenAI TTFT p99 above 2 s for 5 minutes"

      # --- TRAFFIC ---
      - alert: GenAITokenRateLimitApproaching
        expr: |
          genai_provider_rate_limit_utilisation_ratio{env="production"} > 0.80
        for: 3m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Provider token rate limit above 80% for 3 minutes — risk of throttling"

      # --- ERRORS ---
      - alert: GenAIChatErrorRateHigh
        expr: |
          sum(rate(genai_chat_error_total{env="production"}[5m]))
            /
          sum(rate(genai_chat_request_total{env="production"}[5m]))
          > 0.005
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "GenAI chat error rate above 0.5% SLO threshold"

      - alert: GenAIContentFilterRateHigh
        expr: |
          sum(rate(genai_chat_error_total{env="production",error_type="content_filter"}[10m]))
            /
          sum(rate(genai_chat_request_total{env="production"}[10m]))
          > 0.02
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Safety content_filter block rate above 2% — may indicate prompt injection or classifier misconfiguration"

      # --- SATURATION ---
      - alert: GenAIGPUVRAMCritical
        expr: |
          genai_inference_gpu_vram_utilisation_ratio{env="production"} > 0.90
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Self-hosted inference GPU VRAM above 90% — OOM risk imminent"

      - alert: GenAIContextWindowNearlyFull
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.95,
            rate(genai_chat_context_utilisation_ratio_bucket{env="production"}[10m])
          ) > 0.85
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "p95 context utilisation above 85% — approaching context window limit"
The four signals are most useful when read together. A single elevated metric has many possible causes; the combination narrows the diagnosis quickly.
Model performance regression or provider degradation in one region
Traffic spike hitting rate limit ceiling — scale or shed load
Provider outage or authentication failure — check provider status page
Safety classifier over-blocking or token budget too small — check finish_reason distribution
Self-hosted model memory leak or batch accumulation — restart inference pod

The four golden signals — latency, traffic, errors, and saturation — remain the right starting point for GenAI service observability, but each signal must be adapted to the realities of inference workloads. Latency splits into TTFT and end-to-end, with different SLO targets for streaming versus non-streaming paths. Traffic is measured in tokens, not requests. Errors include functional failures like token budget exhaustion and safety blocks that return HTTP 200. Saturation centres on provider rate limits and GPU VRAM rather than CPU. Alert rules built on these adapted signals fire before SLO breaches and provide enough diagnostic context to narrow the incident cause within the first few minutes of triage.
